texts.orig: Post-combustion slipstream CO 2 -capture test facility at Jiangyou Power Plant, Sichuan, China: performance of a membrane separation module under dynamic power-plant operations
texts.text: Post-combustion slipstream CO 2 -capture test facility at Jiangyou Power Plant, Sichuan, China: performance of a membrane separation module under dynamic power-plant operations

texts.orig: Qian Cui 1 , Baodeng Wang 1 , Xinglei Zhao 1 , Guoping Zhang 2 , Zhendong He 2 , Yinhua Long 1 , Yongwei Sun 1  and Anthony Y. Ku 1,3, * ,

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane-based separation technologies have the potential to lower the cost of post-combustion CO 2 capture from power-plant flue gases through reduced energy and capital costs relative to conventional solvent approaches. Studies have shown promise under controlled conditions, but there is a need for data on performance and reliability under field conditions. Coal-fired power plants in China operate in a dynamic manner, with increases and decreases in output causing changes in flue-gas composition. In this paper, we describe the first field test of a membrane-based post-combustion CO 2 -capture system connected to a dynamically operating power plant in China. We report the performance of a Membrane Technology Research, Inc. (MTR) Polaris TM membrane-based capture system over a range of plant operating loads ranging from 54% to 84% and conducted an operational stability test over a 168-h period during which the power plant was operating at an average load of 55%, but ramped as high as 79% and as low as 55%. Our results confirm the ability of a membrane capture system to operate effectively over a wide range of host-plant operating conditions, but also identity several issues related to plant integration, system control and resilience in the face of host-plant upsets that require attention as membrane separation systems move towards commercial use.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  order  to  meet  its  pledge  of  carbon  neutrality  by  2060, China  will  have  to  transform  its  energy  system  from  a heavy reliance  on  coal  to  zero-emissions  sources.  China has already begun a pivot in the power sector, with significant capacity additions in renewable wind and solar generation planned over the 14th Five Year Plan period (2021-25) [1-3]. Dispatchable power will still be needed to ensure grid stability  and  reliability.  In  this  context,  CO 2 capture  and storage (CCS) technologies provide an important option as China moves forward in its transition towards decarbonized energy. CCS technologies will not only need to become more cost-competitive, but also meet the region-specific requirements of CCS in China [4]. For example, CO 2 capture at coal-fired power plants will need to exhibit flexibility in operability  and  deployment  as  the  power  sector  evolves to integrate more intermittent renewable energy. China's path towards CCS may also present opportunities that are different from those in other countries; for example, there may be a greater receptivity to intermediate levels of capture en route to deeper levels of decarbonization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane-based  capture  technologies  could  be  an  attractive option for CO 2 capture at some Chinese power plants. Membranes offer the potential of lower energy use and capital  costs,  relative  to  conventional  solvent-based  methods [5,  6].  Hybrid designs are improving performance and cost potential at high capture rates. In addition, their use of electrical rather than thermal energy for capture offers new opportunities for design integration in air-cooled power plants. Significant progress has been made in membrane materials development, module engineering and performance validation under controlled laboratory settings and pilot testing [7-10]. Notably, MTR completed a multi-year test in 2015 at the US National Carbon Capture Center (NCCC) facility. This test  confirmed the potential of membranes under steadystate  conditions,  but  also  uncovered  challenges  related  to membrane life due to fouling or degradation due to reaction with minor components in the flue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To  reach  their  commercial  potential,  membranes  require additional field testing to understand their capabilities and limitations. For use in the power sector in China, data are needed on how membranes perform under conditions representative of actual use profiles. Practical issues associated  with  host-plant  integration  also  need  to  be identified. To address these questions, we performed slipstream testing of a MTR Polaris TM  membrane CO 2 -capture system at a power plant in China. The paper is organized as follows. We begin with a description of the host power plant and the membrane capture system. Flowsheets are described for the membrane skid and relevant host-plant subsystems. We then discuss the effect of host-plant operation  on  relevant  input  parameters  to  the  membrane separation  system,  membrane  separation  performance over the full range of inlet conditions encountered and an extended test to evaluate stability and fouling potential. We conclude  with  a  discussion  of  lessons  learned  from the testing. This includes a comparison of our results at a Chinese power plant to MTR's testing results at the NCCC, issues  related  to  operational  stability  and  plant  integration, and comments on conceptual and practical issues requiring attention.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Testing was performed at Jiangyou Power Plant in Sichuan province. Four subcritical steam-cycle units are operated at the site, with rated power outputs of 300 MW at two units and 330 MW at the other two units (#33 and #34). Units 33 and 34 are outfitted with selective catalytic reduction technology for NOx control, electrostatic precipitator units and wet flue-gas desulfurization (FGD) units, which have been upgraded to ultra-low emissions. The power plant operates on the provincial grid and ramps seasonally, with higher outputs during the dry season (September to February) and reduced loads during the wet season.

texts.orig: The  details  of  the  slipstream  test  facility  have  been introduced in another paper [11]. The site layout is shown in Fig. 1. Flue gas can be extracted from either Unit 33 or Unit 34, downstream of the FGD unit. The system designs and  operating  procedures  at  these  units  are  nominally identical. The total extracted flow is ≤200 Nm 3 /h, which is <0.02% of the total flue gas from the power plant. After further desulfurization and purification by the pretreatment unit,  the  flue  gas  typically  contains  <5  ppmv  SO 2 and  is routed to test  units. The  depleted  flue  gas  and  captured CO 2 are returned to the stack for venting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A  membrane-based  CO 2 -capture  system  was  purchased from MTR. A simplified process flow diagram of the test skid is shown in Fig. 2. The system comprises a two-stage membrane  separation  process  with  a  recycle  loop.  Both stages  use  standard,  first-generation  Polaris  40'  spiralwound  membrane  modules.  Flue  gas  enters  the  system at  near-ambient  pressure  and  a  blower  is  used  to  overcome  the  pressure  drop  in  the  membrane  vessels  and piping. After leaving the blower, the gas flows through a heat  exchanger  to  cool  the  stream  to  40°C. The  flue  gas then  passes  through  the  gas-liquid  separator  to  remove condensed  water  and  enters  the  first-stage  membrane pressure vessel; electric heat tracing is used to keep the gas at a constant temperature of 40°C as it flowed to the membrane vessel after the separator. The driving force for separation is provided by a permeate vacuum pump. After leaving  the  vacuum  pump,  the  CO 2 -enriched  permeate enters a second membrane stage for further enrichment. This second-stage membrane is also driven by a vacuum pump  and  produces  a  twice-enriched  permeate  stream that contains ~75% CO 2 . Some of this enriched CO 2 can be recycled back to the first membrane stage to increase the CO 2 content. This allows the system to evaluate the effects of recycle loops; it can also be used to artificially simulate different levels of CO 2 in flue gas.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The skid is equipped with a programmable logic controller  control  system  to  allow  automatic  control  of  the system  discharge  pressure  and  the  liquid  level  in  three gas-liquid separators. The flowmeter, pressure gauges and thermometers of the system are displayed locally without a  remote-control function. Data were manually recorded by  operators  at  regular  intervals  during  testing.  In  addition,  the  piping  between  the  #1  separator  and  the  M-1 vessel, the #2 separator and the M-2 vessel, and the M-1 vessel and the M-2 vessel were all equipped with electric heat tracing to ensure that the flue gas entering the membrane vessels was maintained at a constant temperature of ~40°C. The operating ranges and key parameters of the membrane CO 2 -capture system are listed in Table 1.

texts.orig: The operation load of the host plant was tracked during the entire testing campaign. Data were tracked through coordination with the plant control room and reported on a 1-min basis. Host-plant dynamics related to ramping were analysed by computing the load ramp rates from the difference  between  successive  time  steps.  Descriptive  statistics  are  reported  along  with  a  histogram  showing  the distribution of the operating load and ramp rates.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  composition  and  properties  of  the  flue-gas  slipstream were measured in an analysis room. Pressure was measured  using  stainless-steel  vibration-proof  pressure gauges  with  a  measurement  accuracy  of  ±1%. The  temperature of the slipstream was measured using a bimetal thermometer with a  range  of  0-100°C  and  the  measurement accuracy was ±1.5%. The flow rates of the gases were measured using metal-tube rotameters, with uncertainty estimated to be ±0.2%. The composition of the flue gas in the slipstream was measured by using a MSK6300 Fourier Transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, with uncertainty of 2 vol%.  The concentrations of O 2 and particulate matter (PM) were measured before entering the pretreatment system. The pretreatment involves washing the flue gas to remove SO 2 and this process does not change the O 2 content; the PM concentration is sufficiently low at the entrance of the system that washing does not impact the level.

texts.orig: Due  to  limitations  with  the  control  system,  the  concentration  data  were  recorded  manually  every  half  an hour to 2 hours, so they only provide a general trend and reference  range.  The  concentrations  of  CO 2 ,  O2 and  PM were correlated to the host-plant operating load and are reported in graphical form. The effect of ramping on the CO 2 concentration  in  the  flue-gas  stream  was  estimated by  the  differences  from  successive  time  steps.  We  estimate the flue  gas  entering  our  test  skid  lagged  changes in  the  plant  operation  by  ~45  s,  based  on  the  volume  of piping and ductwork from the extraction point for the slipstream to the entrance of the membrane skid of 1.5 m 3  and the  flow  rate  of  120  Nm 3 /hr.  Independent  estimates  of d(CO 2 )/dt using our test data indicate that the CO 2 concentration  changes by <0.01% per minute; consequently, we can treat the membrane as a steady-state system with respect to fluctuations in the feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane  system  was tested from August to November 2020. During the testing period, both continuous and intermittent tests were performed. Continuous tests were  performed  without  restarts  or  stoppage  in  the  operation for the full duration of the test. Intermittent tests were  performed  during  the  day,  with  the  system  shut down  each  night;  a  total  of  ~32  intermittent  tests  were completed  during  the  test  campaign.  The  system  was tested for 72 consecutive hours in late August and 168 consecutive hours in late October; the remaining tests were intermittent tests. The flue gas for the 72-h continuous test was from Unit 33 and the flue gas for the 168-h continuous test was from Unit 34. The filter at the inlet of blower #1 was examined at the end of the 168-h test, after 200 h of cumulative operation.

texts.orig: The total volume of the system (including piping and modules) was ~0.3 m 3 , and the residence time of flue gas in the system was ~54 s based on the maximum flue-gas flow of 20 m 3 /h. The volume of the M-1 vessel is ~0.08 m 3 . The total flow into M-1 is ~24 m 3 /h, the flow on the permeation side is ~7 m 3 /h and the flow on the residual side is ~17 m 3 /h. The residence time of flue gas in M-1 is estimated to be ~12 s. The volume of the M-2 vessel is ~0.04 m 3 . The total flow into M-2 is ~9 m 3 /h, the flow on the permeation side is ~2.5 m 3 /h and the flow on the residual side is ~6.5 m 3 /h. The residence time of flue gas in M-2 is estimated to be ~16 s.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: According  to  the  MTR  experience,  membrane  separation properties are sensitive to temperature. Condensed water can block the membrane material when the temperature is too low and the structure of the membrane material will be damaged when the temperature is too high. We maintained the system at its recommended optimal operating  temperature  of  40°C  using  electric  heat  trace. The feed pressure of the M-2 membrane was generally set to  be  10-15  kPa  higher  than  that  of  the  M-1  membrane. This overcomes the pressure drop of the pipelines so that the pressure when the residual gas of the M-2 membrane returns to the inlet of the M-1 membrane is the same as the  feed  pressure  to  the  M-1  membrane. The  permeate pressure of the M-2 membrane is generally maintained at the lowest pressure.

Condensed water can block the membrane material when the temperature is too low and the structure of the membrane material will be damaged when the temperature is too high. We maintained the system at its recommended optimal operating  temperature  of  40°C  using  electric  heat  trace. The feed pressure of the M-2 membrane was generally set to  be  10-15  kPa  higher  than  that  of  the  M-1  membrane. This overcomes the pressure drop of the pipelines so that the pressure when the residual gas of the M-2 membrane returns to the inlet of the M-1 membrane is the same as the  feed  pressure  to  the  M-1  membrane. The  permeate pressure of the M-2 membrane is generally maintained at the lowest pressure.

texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows a sample calculation for the mass balance used  to  characterize  the  performance  of  the  membrane separation system. The mass flow rate of CO 2 in the mixed gas  was  calculated  using  the  volume  concentration,  the density  and  the  molar  concentration  of  the  mixed  gas under different pressures and temperatures. The density and molar concentration of the gas at different pressures and  temperatures  were  simulated  using  Aspen  Plus®. Sample data were selected from the 168-h test; overall, we observed mass balance closure to within ±5% across our data set. Here, we assume that there is no gas at the outlet of the #2 separator/#3 separator and that the water exits as liquid in the water ring pumps rather than condensed water in the gases. If the flue-gas flow is compared with the  sum  of  the  depleted  gas  and  product  gas  flows,  the mass balance difference of the actual test data closes to within 2.3%.

texts.orig: Sichuan  province  is  rich  in  water  resources  and  hydroelectric  power generation dominates the generation mix during March to August. Coal-fired power is used to meet demand during the autumn and winter dry seasons. During this period, daily ramping of coal plants is not uncommon. Fig. 4a shows the power-plant load during the four-month testing campaign. Insets show the power-plant load during the 72-h and 168-h continuous test runs as Fig. 4b and c, respectively. During our test campaign, the most common power-plant  loads  ranged  from  180  to  190  MW,  but  the plant was operated as low as 177 MW (54% load) and as high as 278 MW (84% load). Unit 33 was shut down from 28 October for maintenance. Daily fluctuations can be seen during the 72-h test. The 168-h test was performed when the power plant was operating at near steady state.

texts.orig: Fig.  5  shows the effect of the power-plant load on the fluegas  composition. The  CO 2 concentration  (Fig.  5a)  ranged from 9.5% to 13.1%, with a decrease in concentration with power-plant  turndown.  The  data  become  noisier  below ~190 MW (58% load), possibly due to less stable control of the boiler under turndown operation. The trend is statistically  significant,  but  the  noise  at  lower  loads  suggests that other factors impact CO 2 as well. The ranges for O 2 , PM and H 2 O content in flue gas under different plant loads are plotted in Fig. 5b-d. The oxygen concentration decreases with the increase in load; when the load increased from 54% to 84%, the oxygen content in the flue gas decreased from ~9.1% to 4.9%. The trend in O 2 with power-plant load can  be  rationalized  in  terms  of  boiler  operation,  where turndown results in either lower combustion rates or more bypass air. From the perspective of post-combustion CO 2 capture, O 2 is undesirable in the captured CO 2 stream because of the complications it can bring in the corrosion of pipelines during transport and oxidation-driven reduction in  permeability  in  subsurface  reservoirs;  specifications for  pipeline corrosion typically limit O 2 concentration by <10  ppm [12].  For  typical  flue-gas  compositions  encountered in this study, this translates into membrane CO 2 /O 2 permselectivity requirements of >40 000 if the membrane system were used to directly achieve the specification. In practice, most membrane designs use a purification step to reach the oxygen target; for example, MTR uses liquefaction of the enriched CO 2 in the permeate to reach the 10-ppm limit. These issues, along with possible membrane degradation through oxidation mechanisms, are noted for further attention in future work.

texts.orig: Fig. 7 shows the effect of feed pressure. At least three tests  were  performed  at  each  of  nine  conditions,  evenly spaced between 135 and 143 kPa. During these tests, the flue-gas flow rate was held steady at the maximum flow rate of 25 kg/h and the permeate pressure was maintained at the lowest pressure of 26 kPa. Fig. 7a plots the CO 2 concentrations in flue gas, depleted gas and product gas over the  range  of  interest.  Increasing  feed  pressure  causes  a weak negative trend in the CO 2 concentration in the depleted gas stream and a weak positive trend in the capture rate. These are consistent with physical expectations related to larger driving forces for permeation across the membrane, but the trends only show marginal statistical significance  due  to  noise  in  the  data  and  the  relatively small pressure range. Fig. 7b shows the effect of feed pressure on the CO 2 -capture rate and specific energy consumption. Increasing the feed pressure by 6% (135 to 143 kPa) results in an increase of 6.3% (67.4% to 73.7%) in the CO 2 -capture rate. The positive trend in the capture rate with increasing  feed  pressure  is  consistent  with  physical  expectations, but it is only marginally statistically significant due to noise in the data. The specific power consumption shows a possible minimum at 141 kPa. This may be due to statistical noise or it may be due to the physical trade-off between the CO 2 -capture rate and the higher power consumption from the blower.

texts.orig: The general trend in the CO 2 -capture rate is expected to continue at higher feed pressures. However, this would require replacement of the blower with a gas compressor, which would lead to a different (and presumably higher) energy consumption. Instead, the as-designed skid uses a vacuum pump on the permeate side to increase the pressure driving force across the membrane. Fig. 8 shows the effect of the permeate pressure at the outlet of M1. In these tests,  the  flue-gas  flow  rate  was  maintained  at  ~23  kg/h and the feed pressure was held constant at 134 kPa. Fig. 8a shows the CO 2 concentration in the flue gas, depleted gas and product gas streams for permeate pressures over the range of 26-46 kPa. As expected, increasing the pressure gradient across the membrane by decreasing the permeate pressure changes the CO 2 concentrations in the streams in  a  manner  consistent  with  improved  separation. This effect can be seen in Fig. 8b in the computed CO 2 -capture rate and the specific power use. The dynamic range of permeate pressures in the membrane capture system is about three times the dynamic range available for feed pressure (viz. 20 vs 8 kPa). Over this larger range available from the permeate pressure tests, it is easier to see stronger trends in separation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Increasing the permeate pressure from 26 to 31 kPa reduced the pressure ratio from 5.2 to 4.3. The CO 2 -capture rate increases with the pressure ratio, leading to a lower CO 2 concentration in the product due to the higher permeation  rates  of  non-CO 2 gases.  However,  continued increases in the permeate pressure from 31 to 46 kPa reduced the pressure ratio from 4.3 to 2.9; this caused the reverse trend in the permeate-both the CO 2 -capture rate and CO 2 concentration in the permeate stream decreased. Over the second range of permeate pressures, the capture rate decreased by 41.6% (from 66.5% to 24.9%) and the CO 2 concentration  in  the  product  decreased  by  17.1%  (from 73.2% to 56.1%). This suggests that when the pressure ratio is >4, the membrane can achieve good separation performance but at pressure ratios <4, the apparent permeability and  selectivity  of  the  membrane  both  decreased  greatly due to the low CO 2 driving force under these conditions. Therefore,  it  is  very  important  to  maintain  an  adequate pressure ratio (increase the feed pressure or decrease the permeate pressure) during membrane-system operation.

However, in our system, the  permeation rate for a given surface area did not increase  enough  to  keep  the  relative  fraction  of  CO 2 permeated through the membrane constant with increasing flow rate. The net effect of increasing the flow through the module was an increase  in  the  average  partial  pressure of CO 2 in the stream. This in turn results in a higher CO 2 concentration in the permeate stream, but a lower average capture rate. Fig. 9 shows these trends using data from a series of tests at flow rates ranging from 12 to 26 kg/h. The feed  and  permeate  pressures  were  held  constant  at  137 and 26 kPa, respectively. The CO 2 -concentration data are shown in Fig. 9a and the membrane-system performance plot  is  presented  in  Fig.  9b. The  fitted  trend  line  for  CO 2 concentration in the product gas concentration shows a weak dependence on the flue-gas flow rate, but the noisiness of the date indicates that this is not statistically significant; there is no physical reason to expect a meaningful trend in these data based on the design of the membrane system. In our system, the specific power consumption decreased with the increasing flow rate because the benefits from a higher absolute quantity of CO 2 captured (despite a lower overall capture rate) outweighed the higher energy use required to drive the higher flow.

texts.orig: A final qualification test was performed by recycling a portion of the CO 2 permeate stream to the feed. These tests allowed  us  to  gain  insight  into  the  system  performance over  a  wider  range  of  feed  CO 2 concentrations.  Results from these tests are shown in Fig. 10. The flow rate was held constant at 25 kg/h and the feed and permeate pressures were 135 and 30 kPa, respectively. Consistently with expectations, increasing feed concentration increased both the CO 2 concentration in the permeate and the overall capture rate. The energy consumption also decreased with increasing concentration.

Fig. 12 shows the influence of the plant load on the system performance during the 72-h and 168-h tests. In the 72-h tests, we selected the continuous  test  data  in  which  the  operating  parameters were  constant  for  24  h.  Other  tests  correspond  to  membrane optimization during the initial stages of testing and do not reflect the influence of the plant load on the system due  to  the  artificial  changes  in  membrane  operation  (e.g. changes in feed pressure, permeate pressure and flue-gas flow rate). During the 24-h test, the feed and permeate pressures  were  fixed  at  138  and  27  kPa,  respectively,  and  the flue-gas  flow  rate  was  fixed  at  the  maximum  of  25  kg/h. In the 168-h tests, we selected the data of the first 120 h when the flow rate and feed pressure were stable to ensure that the  membrane  system  was  run  with  constant  operating parameters; the feed and permeate pressures were fixed at 141 and 21 kPa, respectively, and the flue-gas flow rate was fixed at the maximum of 26 kg/h. The plant output ranged from 180 to 260 MW; the load curves are shown in Fig. 4b and  c.  Fig.  12a  shows  the  CO 2 concentrations  in  the  fluegas feed and retentate streams at different operating loads during the test. The feed concentration ranged from 9.9% to 13%, the product concentration ranged from 70.8% to 77.4% and the retentate concentration ranged from 3% to 4.5%. The data at a given power-plant load are tightly clustered, suggesting that the membrane system performs relatively reproducibly as the plant cycles through different load levels. This behaviour is consistent with the observation that the residence  time  in  the  membrane  system  is  faster  than the rates at which the plant adjusts the load. As a result, the  response  of  the  membrane  system  is  relatively  fast and the system appears to stabilize at each load level quickly. Fig. 12b plots the CO 2 -capture rate and power consumption as a function of different CO 2 concentrations in the feed stream. Consistently with qualification testing results, there is a minor, but discernible improvement in the power consumption when the plant is operating at higher loads. The capture rate was relatively stable across the range of plant operating  loads  and  CO 2 concentrations  in  the  feed.  The scatter at different absolute feed concentrations is comparable to the scatter across the entire range. One interpretation

texts.orig: of this observation is that short-term consequences related to ramping and stabilization of the system after load changes have at least as much of an effect on the capture-rate variability  as  the  absolute  level  of  CO 2 in  the  feed. Additional testing, over longer periods and different ramping profiles, will  be  needed  to  validate  this  hypothesis.  Fig.  12c  shows the  correlations  between  CO 2 concentrations  in  the  feed and product. The CO 2 concentration in the product and the CO 2 concentration in the feed showed a positive correlation, indicating that the membrane system responded faster than the relevant variations in host-plant operation and that the system can be assumed to operate at steady state. In addition, as can be seen from Fig. 12b and c, the 168-h test data and the 72-h test data are highly similar, although the plant load changes more frequently and dramatically during the 72-h test. This suggests that the membrane system is robust to host-plant operation. Fig. 12d shows the correlations between the plant output and CO 2 -capture rate. texts.orig: 4.1   Comparison of our results vs MTR testing at the NCCC
texts.text: 4.1   Comparison of our results vs MTR testing at the NCCC

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The membrane system performed in a manner consistent with its design capability. Our findings are consistent with and complementary to results from an extended test campaign conducted by MTR at the NCCC from 2011 to 2015 [5]. The MTR test used a slipstream from an 880-MW e supercritical pulverized  coal  plant  running  nominally  under  baseload conditions. There were two campaigns performed: an initial test at the 1 ton per day (tpd) scale using a test system with design comparable to the skid that we procured from MTR; and a 20-tpd-scale test using an improved system, equipped with second-generation Polaris membranes. Our test results are mostly directly comparable to the first campaign.
texts.text: The membrane system performed in a manner consistent with its design capability. Our findings are consistent with and complementary to results from an extended test campaign conducted by MTR at the NCCC from 2011 to 2015 [5]. The MTR test used a slipstream from an 880-MW e supercritical pulverized  coal  plant  running  nominally  under  baseload conditions. There were two campaigns performed: an initial test at the 1 ton per day (tpd) scale using a test system with design comparable to the skid that we procured from MTR; and a 20-tpd-scale test using an improved system, equipped with second-generation Polaris membranes. Our test results are mostly directly comparable to the first campaign.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: MTR's 1-tpd test campaign accumulated ~10 000 h of run time, using a flue gas with a feed CO 2 concentration of between 11% and 12% vol. For comparison, the dynamic range in the CO 2 concentration in our tests was 4% over a range of 9.5% to 13.5%. Using a wider range and the presence of active ramping of the host-plant operating load, we  did  not  observe  any  unexpected  responses  in  membrane operation. Instead, the membrane system was able to respond at a rate faster than the changes in the hostplant operation, suggesting that concerns about transients during operation may be manageable.

Using a wider range and the presence of active ramping of the host-plant operating load, we  did  not  observe  any  unexpected  responses  in  membrane operation. texts.orig: MTR's  tests  at  the  NCCC  were  able  to  achieve  higher capture rates (>90% vs >70%) than we achieved during our tests. However, we have not fully optimized the operation of our membrane test system, so this is not considered a salient difference at this point. During our tests, we used an average power consumption of ~224 kWh e /tCO 2 to achieve 70% capture and produce a stream with 75% purity. The energy requirement will increase for higher capture rates and higher product purity. MTR publications indicate energy requirements of 358 kWh e /tCO 2 to produce >99% pure

texts.label: text
texts.orig: More broadly, our results support earlier findings that membrane systems offer advantages over other technologies.  The  energy  requirement  for  membrane  systems  is electrical,  most  conveniently  debited  from  the  net  electrical output of the host plant as an auxiliary load. In contrast, the primary energy requirement in solvent systems is thermal, used in regeneration of the solvent. Assuming a thermal efficiency of 30%, our average energy consumption  of  224  kWh e /tCO 2 corresponds  to  2.7  GJ th /tCO 2 ;  if  a similar  electrical  energy  demand  is  needed  in  supercritical and ultra-supercritical plants operating at 40-50% efficiency, the equivalent energy requirement drops to 1.6-2.0 GJ th /tCO 2 . We note that these results are for a small pilot using relatively inefficient liquid ring pumps. Larger systems would use centrifugal or axial machines with higher efficiency  with  higher  efficiencies,  resulting  in  lower  energy  use.  Some  of  these  benefits  would  be  offset  by  the additional energy required to purify the CO 2 produced by the  two-stage  membrane system. For reference, conventional and state-of-the-art solvent capture systems require from 2.0 to 3.0 GJ th /tCO 2 [15-18].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: First, concerns about the variability in host-plant operation and flue-gas conditions can be manageable. Our data indicate that the responsiveness of the membrane system was  faster  than  the  ramping  dynamics  associated  with load changes in the plant. This suggests that controls for the capture system will need to account for the flue-gas conditions from the host plant, but that feedback effects associated with transients are of limited concern.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Although our data come from a single plant operating in southern China, the dynamic load operation of coal-fired power  plants  in  China  is  well  documented.  During  our study, the fastest ramping events only caused changes in the CO 2 concentration at rates of 0.01%/min (cf. Fig. 8). For typical feed concentrations from coal-fired power plants, this amounts to an ~0.1% change in the partial pressure driving force over 1 min. Our system was designed with residence times of <1 min and we submit that future systems designed with this constraint should also be robust to transients related to power-plant ramping.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Second,  fouling  can  also  be  caused  by  upset  events from the host plant. Previous studies by MTR suggest the long-term fouling of membrane systems, possibly by the gradual  accumulation  of  contaminants  from  upstream emissions-control equipment [5]. Here, we observe the potential for sudden fouling events originating from upsets in the plant; the chemical composition indicates that the material  was  fly  ash.  Upsets  introduce  the  potential  for rapid, potentially irreversible drops in the flow rate. These issues are not insurmountable; simple solutions such as parallel filter systems or flow bypasses can be introduced to build resilience into the system. An example is shown in Fig. 13.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Third, it is desirable to minimize the cost of CO 2 capture.  In  a  real-world  power  plant,  particularly  in  China, variations in the host-plant operation beg the question of how to support the lowest-cost operation of the capture system. Known principles apply-high pressure ratios are favourable,  balanced  with  the  trade-offs  in  energy  consumption  from  operating  blowers  and  vacuum  pumps within different pressure and flow ranges. This issue could be  compounded  when  the  power-plant  operation  varies and the key question is whether to dynamically vary the conditions of the membrane capture system or just 'ride through' plant variations with minimal changes to the operational  set  points.  Our  data  suggest  that  either  option might be viable in China. Not all of the trends in system performance in response to host-plant load are statistically significant above the level of system noise. This suggests  that  it  may  be  simpler  to  operate  the  membrane system under fixed conditions rather than introduce the complexity and effort to dynamically control the system. Further testing and larger data sets, as well as more detailed techno-economic analysis, are needed to determine how generally this observation applies across power plants across China over longer periods of time.

texts.orig: 4.3   Conceptual and practical issues requiring attention
texts.text: 4.3   Conceptual and practical issues requiring attention

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Continued optimization of membrane  materials and system design to reduce cost and improve separation performance is a necessary step towards the commercialization  of  membrane-based  capture  systems.  More  broadly, there is a need to consider the operation of such systems under flexible  conditions.  Flexibility  is  needed  from  two aspects.
texts.text: Continued optimization of membrane  materials and system design to reduce cost and improve separation performance is a necessary step towards the commercialization  of  membrane-based  capture  systems.  More  broadly, there is a need to consider the operation of such systems under flexible  conditions.  Flexibility  is  needed  from  two aspects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The first relates to increasing demands for power plants to  operate  using  non-steady-state  load  profiles.  This  is driven, in part, by increasing levels of renewable wind and solar  generation.  In  liberalized  electricity  markets,  fossil power plants fall lower in the merit order for dispatch and hence  operate  countercyclical  to  wind  and  solar  generation. At the same time, there is growing interest in the use of fossil power plants to provide firm power to support grid reliability. Both of these dynamics are shifting power plants towards 'flexible' operating profiles in which their outputs  are  modulated  rather  than  traditional  baseload profiles [19]. In China, a combination of regulatory preference for renewables dispatch, structural features such as the  hydrological  season  in  Sichuan  and  overcapacity  in coal  generation combine to drive a similar end result in terms of non-steady load profiles [4, 20]. CO 2 -capture systems will need to be compatible with host plants that are operating in a flexible manner.
texts.text: The first relates to increasing demands for power plants to  operate  using  non-steady-state  load  profiles.  This  is driven, in part, by increasing levels of renewable wind and solar  generation.  In  liberalized  electricity  markets,  fossil power plants fall lower in the merit order for dispatch and hence  operate  countercyclical  to  wind  and  solar  generation. At the same time, there is growing interest in the use of fossil power plants to provide firm power to support grid reliability. Both of these dynamics are shifting power plants towards 'flexible' operating profiles in which their outputs  are  modulated  rather  than  traditional  baseload profiles [19]. In China, a combination of regulatory preference for renewables dispatch, structural features such as the  hydrological  season  in  Sichuan  and  overcapacity  in coal  generation combine to drive a similar end result in terms of non-steady load profiles [4, 20]. CO 2 -capture systems will need to be compatible with host plants that are operating in a flexible manner.

texts.orig: A second aspect involves flexible operation of the CO 2 -capture  system.  Here,  interest  in  flexible  capture  rates arises  in  liberalized  electricity  markets  from  the  opportunity to arbitrage electricity prices for economic advantage  [4,  21].  Opportunities  to  take  advantage  of  flexible capture in China are still evolving. Electricity-market reforms  have  created  spot  market  pricing  experiments, but the sufficiently strong market signals needed to optimize flexible capture operation do not yet exist. Moreover, carbon-intensity targets for coal power-plant operators are imminent, but the specific targets remain to be defined at the time of this writing. Ultimately, assessments of flexible operation of membrane capture systems connected to host plants that are also operating in a flexible manner are of  interest,  but  this  is  beyond  the  scope  of  this  work.  In this context, combined flexible operation of both the host plant and the capture system is an area requiring future attention.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study only considered the ability of a membrane system to operate in a steady-state manner under flexible operation of the host plant. Active control of the membrane system allowing it to operate in a flexible manner could be of interest in efforts to optimize the economics and overall carbon intensities of a power plant operating under liberalized electricity markets and carbon constraints.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We  operated  a  membrane-based  post-combustion  CO 2 -capture system at a host plant in China to assess the capability  of  the  system  to  handle  dynamic  situations  that could  be  encountered  under  real-world  conditions.  The system was able to produce a 75% pure CO 2 stream and sustain a 70% capture rate over a continuous 168-h test, during which the host power plant ramped its output from 55% to 79%. These results show that membrane systems can be designed to handle transient host-plant conditions; issues for additional attention have also been identified.
